summary: Spolsky explains why Fog Creek shipped FogBugz 5.0 six months early, tracing the decision back to a psychology insight about subjective well-being: people are happier when they feel in control of their environment. He argues this principle is the most important thing software designers can learn, and applies it to explain why Ajax-powered web interfaces feel so much better than traditional page-reload workflows. After adding Ajax features to FogBugz's most-used pages, the team found the improvements so compelling they decided to ship them immediately rather than wait for the full 5.0 feature set. The result was essentially FogBugz 4½ labeled as 5.0, prioritizing user experience over feature completeness.
key_points: The psychological principle that people's happiness correlates with their sense of control over their environment is the most important insight for software designers

Good design gives users immediate, responsive feedback (iPod click sounds, instant beeps on transactions), while bad design makes users feel ignored (mushy phone buttons, disabled DVD menu buttons, endless security popups)
Ajax techniques create happiness by giving users instant feedback instead of waiting for server round-trips, making web apps feel more like native applications
Fog Creek dogfooded the Ajax improvements internally and found them so compelling they shipped early with just those features rather than waiting for the full planned release
The Ajax changes to FogBugz's two most-used pages cut server round-trips roughly in half, with keyboard shortcuts adding further speed improvements
money_quotes: Put people in direct control of the stuff around them and they will, more or less, on average, be happier.

If you're a software designer, this is it. This is your big chance to do something meaningful to improve the world. Design software that puts the user in control and you'll increase happiness, even if your product is the most boring accounting software imaginable.
Repeated failure inhibits the experience of agency, decreasing subjective well-being.

Web browsers deal with the security problem by displaying a seemingly endless series of modal popups asking you to confirm that you really want to have three NFL linebackers come into your home and force-feed you a football.

The net result is faster, crisper feedback that makes you feel in control and creates subjective well-being, a.k.a. happiness, a feeling that is biochemically NO DIFFERENT THAN EATING LARGE QUANTITIES OF CHOCOLATE.
It's really pretty cool to be able to work through a bunch of cases, assigning, editing, and reprioritizing, without ever reaching for the mouse.
We're only on year six of the 'great software takes ten years' rule, but I'd say we're more than 60% there.
key_insight: The most impactful thing a software designer can do is give users a sense of control through responsive, immediate feedback — because perceived agency directly creates happiness.
